audit_status.source_base: Greek Philemon text tradition, draft aligned to public-domain comparison layer later
audit_status.review_note: Complete one-chapter draft reviewed for appeal rather than command, Onesimus as beloved sibling, partnership, debt language, anti-slavery dignity safeguards.

verses.restored: Paul, prisoner of Messiah Jesus, and Timothy the sibling, to Philemon our beloved and coworker,
and to Apphia the sister, and to Archippus our fellow soldier, and to the assembly in your house:
Grace to you and peace from Divine our Father and Lord Jesus Messiah.
I give thanks to my Divine always, making remembrance of you in my prayers,
hearing of your love and the trust that you have toward the Lord Jesus and toward all the holy ones,
so that the partnership/fellowship of your trust may become effective in full knowledge of every good thing in us into Messiah.
For I had much joy and comfort because of your love, because the inward affections of the holy ones have been refreshed through you, sibling.
Therefore, having much boldness in Messiah to command you what is fitting,
yet because of love I rather appeal, being such a one as Paul, an elder, and now also prisoner of Messiah Jesus.
I appeal to you concerning my child, whom I begot in chains, Onesimus,
the one formerly useless to you but now useful both to you and to me,
whom I sent back to you—him, that is, my own inward affections.
I wished to keep him with myself, so that on your behalf he might serve me in the chains of the good news,
but apart from your consent I wanted to do nothing, so that your good might not be according to compulsion but according to willingness.
For perhaps because of this he was separated for an hour, so that you might have him back forever,
no longer as enslaved, but beyond enslaved: a beloved sibling, especially to me, and how much more to you, both in flesh and in the Lord.
Therefore, if you hold me as partner, receive him as me.
And if he wronged you in anything or owes, charge this to me.
I, Paul, write with my own hand: I will repay, not to say to you that you owe me even yourself.
Yes, sibling, may I benefit from you in the Lord; refresh my inward affections in Messiah.
Having been persuaded of your obedience, I write to you, knowing that you will do even beyond what I say.
At the same time also prepare a guest room for me, for I hope that through your prayers I will be graciously given to you.
Epaphras, my fellow prisoner in Messiah Jesus, greets you,
as do Mark, Aristarchus, Demas, Luke, my coworkers.
The grace of the Lord Jesus Messiah be with your spirit.

safety_note: Philemon must never be used to sanctify slavery. The text is an appeal that pressures Philemon toward receiving Onesimus no longer as enslaved but beyond enslaved, as beloved sibling. The source-audit trajectory should preserve Onesimus' dignity, agency, and siblinghood and flag the letter for deep reception-history audit.
translation_notes: Koinonos/koinonia language is rendered partner/partnership to foreground shared participation.
Paul appeals rather than commands, but the appeal is morally forceful.
Verse 16 is the hinge: no longer as enslaved, but beyond enslaved: beloved sibling.
